I've worked on a couple of projects that I'm quite proud of.

results.rawResponse:
I have built two primary machine learning projects that focus on evaluation and recommendation systems.

The first is LLM-Eval, which is an open-source framework I developed using Python, PyTorch, and Hugging Face to evaluate language model performance across fifteen benchmarks. It has actually grown to about fifteen hundred GitHub stars. When building this, I wanted to address the variance in LLM evaluation, similar to the methodology challenges discussed in papers like HELM, ensuring we get reproducible zero-shot and few-shot metric evaluations.

The second is RecSys-Factory, a production-grade recommendation library built with Python, TensorFlow, and Redis. It focuses heavily on multi-armed bandit exploration strategies. I implemented algorithms like Thompson Sampling and Upper Confidence Bound, which are standard in literature for handling the cold-start problem and balancing the classic exploration-exploitation trade-off in real-time production environments.
results.normalizedResponse:
i have built two primary machine learning projects that focus on evaluation and recommendation systems.

the first is llm-eval, which is an open-source framework i developed using python, pytorch, and hugging face to evaluate language model performance across fifteen benchmarks. it has actually grown to about fifteen hundred github stars. when building this, i wanted to address the variance in llm evaluation, similar to the methodology challenges discussed in papers like helm, ensuring we get reproducible zero-shot and few-shot metric evaluations.

the second is recsys-factory, a production-grade recommendation library built with python, tensorflow, and redis. it focuses heavily on multi-armed bandit exploration strategies. i implemented algorithms like thompson sampling and upper confidence bound, which are standard in literature for handling the cold-start problem and balancing the classic exploration-exploitation trade-off in real-time production environments.

results.rawResponse:
I can walk you through RecSys-Factory. It is a production recommendation library I built to address the user cold-start problem using multi-armed bandit strategies. 

I focused on implementing contextual bandit algorithms, specifically LinUCB, which is based on the foundational paper by Li and colleagues on personalized recommendation. This approach dynamically balances exploration and exploitation in real-time based on user features. For the architecture, I used TensorFlow to train and generate offline user and item embeddings, and integrated Redis to serve those embeddings and update the exploitation-exploration states with sub-ten millisecond latency.

results.rawResponse:
When approaching cold outreach for a new market, I'd start by researching the market and identifying key players, trends, and pain points. So, I've done this in previous roles, and my approach has been to use a combination of LinkedIn, industry reports, and news articles to get a sense of the landscape.

I'd then craft a personalized email or LinkedIn message that speaks directly to those pain points and shows I've taken the time to understand their business. For example, I might say, "I came across [Company Name] and noticed you're expanding into [specific area]. I've had success helping similar companies in [previous market] and thought we could explore potential synergies."

The key is to be specific, relevant, and concise. I'd focus on the value I can bring, rather than just listing features or services. So, my opening line might be, "Hi [First Name], I'm Marcus from [Company Name]. I help [specific type of company] like yours drive growth through [specific area of expertise]."

From there, I'd follow up with a clear call-to-action, like scheduling a call to discuss further. And I'd make sure to track my outreach efforts, so I can refine my approach and improve results over time.

I basically focused on making rollbacks as automated and metrics-driven as possible. This approach kept our mean time to resolution very low and shielded users from bad deploys before a human even had to intervene.

results.rawResponse: I have worked on two key security projects. The first is ThreatHunter-Playbook, which is a community-contributed Sigma rule library with over 500 rules mapped to various MITRE ATT&CK techniques using Splunk and YARA. The other is PhishDetect, an ML-based email phishing detection tool built with Python and TensorFlow that incorporates DMARC and SPF to achieve 94% precision, and it is currently used by three MSSPs.
results.normalizedResponse: i have worked on two key security projects. the first is threathunter-playbook, which is a community-contributed sigma rule library with over 500 rules mapped to various mitre att&ck techniques using splunk and yara. the other is phishdetect, an ml-based email phishing detection tool built with python and tensorflow that incorporates dmarc and spf to achieve 94% precision, and it is currently used by three mssps.

results.rawResponse: When I investigate a suspicious login alert, my first step is always to pull the telemetry and establish a baseline for that specific user. I map this behavior to MITRE ATT&CK techniques like Valid Accounts, T1078, or Brute Force, T1110, to categorize the vector. From there, I analyze the IP reputation, check for impossible travel indicators, and verify if the device matches their enrolled profile. Finally, I pivot to look for downstream actions like MFA registration changes or unexpected data access to determine if the credential was actually compromised.
